skill.name: email-notify
skill.description: Send SMTP email notifications when Codex, Claude, or Claude Code finishes a task. The helper includes device, project, status, and summary details in the message.
skill.summary: Send task completion emails through a user configured SMTP account.
skill.author: caopulan

security_audit.summary: Static findings for network, environment access, filesystem references, and documented commands are mostly expected for an SMTP notification skill. No prompt injection or malicious intent was found, but the skill can email task summaries and uses SMTP credentials from environment variables, so users need a clear data disclosure warning.

medium_findings.title: Task Summary Can Be Sent Over SMTP
medium_findings.description: The helper sends email through smtplib and includes the task title, status, project, device name, and summary. This is the intended feature, but it can disclose sensitive task details if the summary contains secrets or private project information.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly constructs an email body from task metadata and sends it through SMTP. This confirms network transmission, while the purpose appears legitimate and user configured.

medium_findings.title: SMTP Credentials Are Read From Environment Variables
medium_findings.description: The script reads SMTP host, username, password, sender, recipients, and TLS settings from environment variables. This is normal for SMTP configuration, but credentials can be exposed if shell profiles or logs are mishandled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly reads CODEX_EMAIL_PASSWORD and uses it for SMTP login. The behavior is required for authenticated SMTP, but it handles sensitive credentials.

medium_findings.title: Plaintext Email Password Configuration Is Recommended
medium_findings.description: The instructions tell users to place CODEX_EMAIL_PASSWORD in shell startup files. This is easy to use but increases exposure to local users, backups, terminal history, and accidental sharing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly recommends persistent environment variables in home shell configuration files. The risk is credential exposure, not malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: AGENTS.md Project Name Read Is Limited
low_findings.description: The script searches upward for AGENTS.md and reads it to extract a project name. This is filesystem access, but it is limited to project metadata and no file contents are emailed except the extracted name.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem access is explicit and narrow. It could reveal a project name in email, but I did not find evidence of broader file exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Documented Command Is Not Dynamic Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The command in SKILL.md is a user-facing example for running the helper script. I did not find code that builds or executes shell commands dynamically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is a Markdown command example, not executable code inside the skill. The Python script uses argparse and smtplib, not subprocess or shell evaluation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is Not Supported By Evidence
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography static finding appears to be triggered by SMTP text, not by a weak hash, cipher, or custom cryptographic implementation. The script uses standard SMTP, SMTP_SSL, and STARTTLS controls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No weak algorithm or cryptographic primitive is present at the flagged locations. The remaining concern is configuration quality, especially if users disable TLS and SSL.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Is Not Supported By Evidence
low_findings.description: The flagged lines validate boolean environment values and tell users to avoid secrets in summaries. I did not find host probing, process listing, network scanning, or system inventory collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code only parses configured environment flags and formats user-provided task metadata. No reconnaissance behavior is visible in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Hidden Home Path References Are Installation Documentation
low_findings.description: The skill mentions shell startup files and a ~/.codex skill path. These references are expected for local configuration, but users should protect files containing credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references are documentation rather than hidden-file reads by code. They still matter because the documented files may store SMTP credentials.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Transmission With User-Supplied Summary
dangerous_patterns.description: The summary argument is inserted into the email body and sent to configured recipients. Users must avoid including secrets, credentials, or private records in summaries.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from command-line summary to email body to SMTP send is direct. It is intended behavior but a real disclosure path.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Use For Outbound SMTP
dangerous_patterns.description: The script reads SMTP credentials from environment variables and logs into the configured server. This should use app passwords or a secret manager where possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Credential variables are clearly read and used for SMTP authentication. No evidence shows the password is emailed or printed.

content.value_statement: Long-running agent tasks can finish when users are away from the terminal. This skill sends a concise SMTP email with device, project, status, and summary details.
actual_capabilities: Sends SMTP email notifications after a task completes.
Includes device name, project name, status, and summary in the email body.
Reads the project name from AGENTS.md, a command option, or the current folder.
Supports multiple recipients separated by commas or semicolons.
Supports authenticated SMTP, unauthenticated SMTP, STARTTLS, SMTP over SSL, timeouts, and dry runs.
limitations: Requires a working SMTP server and correct environment variables.
Does not remove secrets from task summaries automatically.
Does not provide delivery tracking, retries, or queueing.
Stores SMTP settings in the user environment unless the user chooses another secret workflow.

use_cases.title: Notify After Long Builds
use_cases.description: Send a completion email when Codex finishes tests, builds, or migrations that take several minutes.

use_cases.title: Report Agent Run Results
use_cases.description: Email a short status and summary after an agent completes a requested code change.

use_cases.title: Track Remote Workflows
use_cases.description: Receive task completion alerts from a remote machine without staying connected to the terminal.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Completion Email
prompt_templates.prompt: When this task is finished, use email-notify to send me a success or failure email with a short summary.

prompt_templates.title: Custom Status Message
prompt_templates.scenario: Add a clear status and task title.
prompt_templates.prompt: After the run, send an email notification titled "Dependency update" with the final status and a concise summary.

prompt_templates.title: Dry Run SMTP Check
prompt_templates.scenario: Confirm settings before sending real email.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use email-notify in dry-run mode to verify the SMTP host, recipients, subject, and body before sending a real notification.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Workflow Alert
prompt_templates.scenario: Use explicit project and detailed result context.
prompt_templates.prompt: When the deployment checks complete, send an email for project "Billing API" with status partial if any check fails, and summarize the next required action.

output_examples.input: Notify after a successful test run for the API project.
output_examples.output: An email is sent with subject "API tests complete" and a body listing the device, project, success status, and summary.

output_examples.input: Run a dry check before enabling real notifications.
output_examples.output: The terminal shows the SMTP host, port, security mode, sender, recipients, subject, and email body without sending mail.

output_examples.input: Notify when a task is blocked by missing credentials.
output_examples.output: An email is sent with blocked status and a short summary that excludes passwords, tokens, and private data.

best_practices: Use app-specific SMTP passwords and rotate them regularly.
Keep summaries short and exclude secrets, tokens, customer data, and private logs.
Test configuration with dry-run before sending real notifications.
anti_patterns: Do not include credentials or private file contents in the summary.
Do not send notifications to broad mailing lists without review.
Do not enable both STARTTLS and SMTP over SSL at the same time.

faq.question: Does this skill send email automatically?
faq.answer: It sends email when the agent follows the skill instructions and runs the helper script.

faq.question: What email provider does it support?
faq.answer: It supports SMTP providers that accept the configured host, port, username, password, and security mode.

faq.question: Can it notify multiple recipients?
faq.answer: Yes. The recipient variable accepts comma-separated or semicolon-separated addresses.

faq.question: Can it work without SMTP authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. Leave the username and password unset if the SMTP server does not require authentication.

faq.question: Does it protect secrets in summaries?
faq.answer: No. Users and agents must avoid placing secrets or private data in task summaries.

faq.question: Can I preview the notification?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper supports dry-run mode to print the message details without sending email.
